metadata.from: Bob (Robert) Hanifen — sender is Pvt. Bob Hanifen at Chanute Field, Illinois
metadata.to: Hanifen Body & Paint Co. / James Edward Hanifen Sr. (Dad)
metadata.date: Jun. 1900 [likely Jun. 19:00, i.e., written at 7:00 PM on a June date, or the '1900' is military time for 7:00 PM]
metadata.location_written_from: Chanute Field, Illinois (per return address and postmark: Rantoul, Ill.)
metadata.location_sent_to: Hanifen Body & Paint Co., 914 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa
metadata.envelope_notes: Return address: Pvt. Bob Hanifen 17170600, Sqd. R - B K S. 604, Chanute Fld. Ill. Marked 'Free' in upper right (military free mail). The date written on the letter appears to be 'Jun. 1900' — likely military time notation meaning he wrote it at 1900 hours (7:00 PM) in June.
people_mentioned: Lt. (Lieutenant, mentioned briefly as 'Lt.' in cockpit)
places_mentioned: Curtis Wright Air Port (18 miles out of St. Louis)
metadata.mood_tone: Excited, enthusiastic, and vivid — Bob is thrilled by his first cross-country flight experience and describes it in energetic detail, with some moments of mild anxiety (hydraulic leak) and humor about air sickness.
metadata.legibility_notes: Generally readable cursive but some words are difficult — 'Lt.' in cockpit scene is partially obscured, some crossed-out words on page 2 and 3, '1900' on date line may be military time rather than a year, page 3 ends mid-sentence.
metadata.historical_context: WWII era — Bob is a private stationed at Chanute Field (Army Air Forces technical training base in Rantoul, Illinois). He describes a cross-country flight to the Curtis Wright Airport near St. Louis, mentions the Mississippi River flooding, and flies with a Major and a Captain. The 'Free' frank on the envelope indicates military free mail privilege.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Letter is dated 'Jun. 1900' (military time for 7:00 PM in June) with Chanute Field return address (postmark Rantoul, Ill.). Bob describes flying to St. Louis in a C-47 — the same flight described in Letter 54 (dated Sun. 0130, which by context is the night before the flight) and Letter 78 (dated Mon. 1900, describing KP after the flight). Letter 54 is the Sunday night before; Letter 53 is written the same day as the flight (a Monday), and Letter 78 is the following Monday evening after KP. The C-47 flight to Curtis Wright Airport near St. Louis is the connecting event. Letter 54 mentions school starting July 2, and the flight occurs before that. Estimated June 25, 1945 (Monday).

pages.transcription:
Jun. 1900

Dear Dad

    Just sitting in my bunk now. Had a swell day. Went down to Operations and got our chutes and took off for St. Louis. We took off at 10 till 1:00 and the thrill was really great. The field passed away very quickly and we started climbing for altitude we leveled off a 9000 ft. We were doing around 160 M.P.H. The field looked very pretty up that high and the road and cars so very mall [small?]. The noise was pretty great, but not too bad. The clouds were pretty thick and they just seemed to melt when we went thru them. We up to the [Lt. in] Cockpit

and talked to the Lt. a while. He was a swell Joe. The air wasn't very rough [at?] but each little down draft or up draft your stomach would drop a foot it seemed. The hyd. fluid started leaking out the landing gear and the Ft. Engineering had a time up there fixing it. Sort of scared me a little a bit.
We went over the Miss. and it was flooding all over the country side it seemed. We circled to land and I about [illegible] — because he really whirled the plane around in a beautiful bank and your stomach really fell funny when you come down from that [crossed out words] height. We came in pretty

fast and the tires really squealed when we hit. The time was 10 min. till 2:00. That is really moving along. We spent 30 min. at the Curtis Wright Air Port, 18 miles out of St. Louis. I didn't feel to [too] good so I never eat anything. It was all free too. Seen a lot of swell planes down there. We left at 10 till three and flew at 4,000 ft. back. It was pretty bumpy, brought back a Major and a Capt. I slept most of the way and I felt pretty good when we made our traffic pattern to land. We landed at 10 till 4:00. I felt darns good. I guess I was excited going down there. Eat a good kennen [dinner?] and now I find out

full_transcription:
Jun. 1900

Dear Dad

    Just sitting in my bunk now. Had a swell day. Went down to Operations and got our chutes and took off for St. Louis. We took off at 10 till 1:00 and the thrill was really great. The field passed away very quickly and we started climbing for altitude we leveled off a 9000 ft. We were doing around 160 M.P.H. The field looked very pretty up that high and the road and cars so very mall [small?]. The noise was pretty great, but not too bad. The clouds were pretty thick and they just seemed to melt when we went thru them. We up to the [Lt. in] Cockpit and talked to the Lt. a while. He was a swell Joe. The air wasn't very rough [at?] but each little down draft or up draft your stomach would drop a foot it seemed. The hyd. fluid started leaking out the landing gear and the Ft. Engineering had a time up there fixing it. Sort of scared me a little a bit.
We went over the Miss. and it was flooding all over the country side it seemed. We circled to land and I about [illegible] — because he really whirled the plane around in a beautiful bank and your stomach really fell funny when you come down from that [crossed out words] height. We came in pretty fast and the tires really squealed when we hit. The time was 10 min. till 2:00. That is really moving along. We spent 30 min. at the Curtis Wright Air Port, 18 miles out of St. Louis. I didn't feel to [too] good so I never eat anything. It was all free too. Seen a lot of swell planes down there. We left at 10 till three and flew at 4,000 ft. back. It was pretty bumpy, brought back a Major and a Capt. I slept most of the way and I felt pretty good when we made our traffic pattern to land. We landed at 10 till 4:00. I felt darns good. I guess I was excited going down there. Eat a good kennen [dinner?] and now I find out
summary: Bob Hanifen writes to his father from Chanute Field, Illinois, describing an exciting cross-country flight to St. Louis. He details the thrill of taking off, leveling at 9,000 feet at 160 MPH, flying through thick clouds, and visiting the Curtis Wright Airport 18 miles outside St. Louis. He mentions a brief scare when hydraulic fluid started leaking from the landing gear, witnessing the Mississippi River flooding, and the stomach-dropping sensation of banking turns; the letter ends mid-sentence on page 3, apparently incomplete.
